frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
generated_comment: I don't understand why everyone is getting so upset. Colleges have always had rules and discipline is the foundation of any good institution. These girls are there to study, not to make a fashion statement — jeans and sleeveless tops are for malls and parties, not classrooms. When we were young, there was a certain dignity in how one dressed and that reflected on the family also. Today if we say anything we are called 'regressive' but just look at what happens in societies where there are no standards — respect for women actually goes DOWN, not up. The college is doing a service to these students whether they understand it or not. Salute to the management for having some backbone. 👏
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes paternalistic control over women's bodies under the guise of institutional discipline and cultural values, implying that women's clothing choices invite disrespect and that external regulation of female dress is protective rather than restrictive. It naturalises the idea that women's sartorial autonomy must be managed by institutions and families to maintain social order, a classic covert misogynistic framing that presents patriarchal control as benevolent stewardship.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
